title: How to apply for Disability Living Allowance (DLA) for autistic children
description_plain: Complete guide to applying for DLA for children under 16 with autism, including eligibility, rates, evidence needed, and how to complete the application form.
steps_summary:
1. Check eligibility (under 16, needs more care/supervision than same-age children, needs lasted 3 months)
2. Call DWP 0800 121 4600 to request DLA1 Child form
3. Complete the form in detail - explain how autism affects daily life, use real examples, compare to typical children
4. Gather supporting evidence (diagnosis letters, school reports, therapy assessments, EHCP)
5. Keep a diary for 1-2 weeks showing care provided throughout day and night
6. Mail completed form to: Disability Living Allowance Unit, Post Handling Site B, Wolverhampton WV99 1BY
7. Await decision (6-8 weeks processing time)
eligibility_summary: Children under 16 with autism who need substantially more care or supervision than non-autistic children of the same age. Needs must have lasted 3 months and be expected to continue for 6+ months. No formal diagnosis required but helpful. Not means-tested - income and savings don't affect eligibility.
evidence_required: autism diagnosis letter, paediatrician reports, school support plans or EHCP, speech and language therapy reports, occupational therapy assessments, GP letters, teacher statements, care diary showing support provided
deadlines: No application deadline but payment only backdated from date of claim. Processing takes 6-8 weeks. If refused, request Mandatory Reconsideration within 1 month of decision.
legal_basis: Social Security Contributions and Benefits Act 1992; Disability Living Allowance Regulations 1991
notes_for_maya: Emphasise that no diagnosis is required but functional impact must be clear. Stress importance of detailed examples comparing to typical children. DLA higher rate mobility can qualify for Motability Scheme. At age 16, child must apply for PIP separately.
content_excerpt: DLA helps with the extra costs of caring for a child with a disability or health condition. Care component: £28.70-£108.55/week. Mobility component: £28.70-£75.75/week.

title: How to claim Carer's Allowance for caring for an autistic child
description_plain: Step-by-step guide to claiming Carer's Allowance when you care for an autistic child or adult for 35+ hours per week. Includes 2025 rate increase and new earnings limit.
steps_summary:
1. Ensure the person you care for gets DLA middle/high rate care OR PIP daily living component
2. Confirm you care for them at least 35 hours per week
3. Check earnings limit - must earn £196 or less per week after tax (increased April 2025)
4. Apply online at gov.uk/carers-allowance/how-to-claim (takes 30 minutes)
5. Provide National Insurance numbers for you and person you care for
6. Submit their DLA or PIP award letter details
7. Provide bank details and employment/earnings information
8. Await decision (usually 8-12 weeks)
eligibility_summary: You must be 16 or older, care for someone at least 35 hours per week, and they must receive DLA middle/highest care rate or PIP daily living component. You must earn £196 or less per week after tax and not be in full-time education (21+ hours). Partner's income and household savings don't affect eligibility.
evidence_required: your National Insurance number, person's DLA or PIP award letter, bank details, employment and earnings details (payslips if applicable), person you care for's National Insurance number and date of birth
deadlines: No application deadline but payment only backdated from date of claim. Apply as soon as eligible. Report changes in circumstances within 3 weeks (earnings, care hours, person's benefit status).
legal_basis: Social Security Contributions and Benefits Act 1992; Carer's Allowance Regulations 1976
notes_for_maya: NEW 2025: Earnings limit increased from £151 to £196/week from April 2025. This allows 60,000+ more carers to qualify. Only one person can claim for same child. Carer's Allowance counts as income for UC but you get National Insurance credits. May also qualify for Carer's Element in UC (£198.31/month extra).
content_excerpt: Carer's Allowance is £83.30 per week (2025 rate). You could get it if you care for someone at least 35 hours a week and they get certain benefits.

title: Universal Credit LCWRA (Limited Capability for Work) for autism
description_plain: How to claim the LCWRA element of Universal Credit if autism prevents you from working or doing work-related activity. Includes urgent 2026 deadline for higher rate.
steps_summary:
1. Start Universal Credit claim online at gov.uk/apply-universal-credit
2. Inform DWP about your autism/disability at application stage
3. Submit medical evidence (fit notes, autism diagnosis, professional reports)
4. Request Work Capability Assessment (WCA)
5. Complete UC50 capability for work questionnaire - explain all difficulties in detail
6. Attend WCA appointment (face-to-face, phone, or video) or request home visit
7. If assessed as LCWRA, extra payment starts 3 months after you first submitted medical evidence
8. URGENT: Apply before 6 April 2026 to secure higher rate (£423.27/month vs £217.26/month after this date)
eligibility_summary: Available if autism or related conditions mean you have limited capability for work and work-related activity. Assessment considers social interaction difficulties, coping with change, managing anxiety, sensory processing challenges, and communication barriers. Must be claiming Universal Credit and provide medical evidence of functional limitations.
evidence_required: GP fit notes, autism diagnostic reports, psychiatrist/psychologist reports, occupational therapy assessments, support worker statements, social worker letters, PIP award letters if applicable, evidence of how autism affects daily activities and ability to work
deadlines: CRITICAL: Apply before 6 April 2026 to receive £423.27/month. After this date, new LCWRA claims only get £217.26/month. Existing claimants before April 2026 protected at higher rate. Complete UC50 form within 1 month of receiving it.
legal_basis: Welfare Reform Act 2012; Universal Credit Regulations 2013; Work Capability Assessment descriptors
notes_for_maya: URGENT ACTION NEEDED: Government reducing LCWRA payment by 50% from April 2026 for new claims. This is a major welfare cut affecting autistic people. Strongly emphasise the April 2026 deadline. If user hasn't applied yet, stress urgency. WCA considers autism-specific issues like social interaction, routine changes, sensory overload.
breadcrumb: Benefits › Universal Credit › Health conditions and disability
content_excerpt: If you have a disability or health condition that affects how much you can work, you may be able to get extra money on top of your Universal Credit standard allowance.

title: Council Tax Reduction and discounts for disabled residents in Hounslow
description_plain: Complete guide to Council Tax reductions available in Hounslow for disabled residents, including Severely Mentally Impaired exemption, Disabled Band Reduction, and low-income support.
steps_summary:
1. Identify which reduction you qualify for (SMI exemption, disabled band reduction, or low-income support)
2. Register or sign in to your Council Tax account (use account number and online key from your bill)
3. For SMI exemption: Get medical certificate from doctor confirming severe mental impairment
4. For disabled band reduction: Check property has extra room/bathroom/wheelchair space for disability needs
5. Submit application with supporting documents (medical certificates, benefit letters, property details)
6. For low-income support: Apply via Council Tax Support Scheme
7. Await decision and adjusted Council Tax bill
eligibility_summary: SMI Exemption: All adults in property are severely mentally impaired (permanent) AND receive qualifying benefits (PIP, DLA, etc). Full exemption or 50% discount. Disabled Band Reduction: Property has extra room, bathroom/kitchen, or wheelchair space needed for disability. Reduces band by one level. Council Tax Support: Working-age residents (18-65) on low income or benefits.
evidence_required: For SMI: medical certificate from registered doctor, benefit award letters (PIP/DLA/AA). For disabled band reduction: evidence of disability-related property features. For all: Council Tax account number, proof of residence, benefit documentation
deadlines: Apply as soon as eligible - reductions not automatically backdated beyond application date. SMI exemption requires renewal if circumstances change. Council Tax Support Scheme changes effective April 2025.

contacts.address: Council Tax Team, Hounslow Council, Civic Centre, Lampton Road, Hounslow TW3 4DN

legal_basis: Local Government Finance Act 1992; Council Tax (Reductions for Disabilities) Regulations 1992; Hounslow Council Tax Reduction Scheme 2025-26
notes_for_maya: Hounslow-specific. Three separate reduction types - make clear which applies. SMI exemption can mean £0 Council Tax if all adults severely impaired. Band reduction saves around £200-400/year. Council Tax Support is means-tested. All require separate applications.
content_excerpt: You may be able to get a reduction on your Council Tax if you or someone in your household is disabled. This includes Severely Mentally Impaired exemption and disabled band reduction.

title: Hounslow Discretionary Local Crisis Payments for emergency support
description_plain: Emergency financial support from Hounslow Council for residents facing crisis situations, including families with disabled or autistic members. Covers housing payments, household items, fuel, food, and travel.
steps_summary:
1. Identify if you're facing eligible crisis (homelessness threat, family breakdown, illness, disabled family member needs, emergency expenses)
2. Check you've exhausted other financial support options first
3. Ensure you're on government benefits or low income
4. Call Hounslow Council 020 8583 2000 to discuss your situation
5. Explain your crisis and how it relates to autism/disability needs
6. Provide evidence of circumstances (benefit letters, eviction notice, medical letters, bills)
7. Council assesses application on case-by-case basis (all awards discretionary)
8. If approved, receive payment or vouchers for specific items
eligibility_summary: Hounslow resident or working in the borough, on government benefits or low income, facing exceptional pressure such as illness, disabled family member, homelessness threat, or family breakdown. Must have exhausted other financial aid options. All awards are discretionary and assessed individually.
evidence_required: proof of Hounslow residency, benefit award letters, evidence of crisis (eviction notice, medical letters, funeral costs, emergency bills), evidence of disability or autism diagnosis if relevant to crisis, proof of income/financial situation
deadlines: Apply as soon as crisis occurs - support is for emergencies. No backdating. Council assesses urgency as part of application. Processing time varies by case complexity.

contacts.address: Hounslow Council, Civic Centre, Lampton Road, Hounslow TW3 4DN

legal_basis: Local Government Act 1972; Localism Act 2011; Hounslow Discretionary Crisis Payment Policy
notes_for_maya: Hounslow-specific emergency fund. All awards discretionary - no entitlement. Can cover rent arrears, furniture, white goods, fuel reconnection, food vouchers, travel to hospital/funerals. Particularly relevant for autism families facing meltdowns/crisis, sensory equipment needs, or disability-related emergencies. Also signpost to Citizens Advice Hounslow, Autism Hounslow community group, and Family Hubs for ongoing support.
content_excerpt: If you are facing a crisis and don't have access to any other financial help, you may be able to apply for discretionary local crisis payment support.

title: How to apply for the Motability Scheme with PIP or DLA
description_plain: Complete guide to accessing the Motability Scheme to lease a car, wheelchair-accessible vehicle, scooter, or powered wheelchair using your PIP or DLA mobility component.
steps_summary:
1. Ensure you receive enhanced rate PIP mobility OR higher rate DLA mobility with at least 12 months remaining
2. Check you want to exchange your mobility allowance for a leased vehicle (currently £75.75/week in 2025)
3. Visit motability.co.uk or call 0300 456 4566 to explore vehicles within your allowance
4. Choose vehicle and book appointment at Motability Scheme dealer
5. Bring proof of benefit entitlement (PIP/DLA award letter)
6. Sign lease agreement (3 or 5 years typically)
7. Your mobility allowance payments go directly to Motability - you don't handle payments
8. Receive vehicle with insurance, servicing, breakdown cover, and tyres all included
9. Nominate up to 2 additional named drivers (can include parents/carers)
eligibility_summary: Must receive enhanced rate mobility component of PIP (£75.75/week) OR higher rate mobility component of DLA (£75.75/week) with at least 12 months award remaining. You don't need to be the driver - parents, guardians, or carers can drive on behalf of child or disabled person. Available in England, Wales, Scotland, and Northern Ireland.
evidence_required: PIP or DLA award letter showing enhanced/higher rate mobility component, proof of identity, driving licence details for nominated drivers (if applicable), proof of address
deadlines: Apply any time while receiving qualifying benefit with 12+ months remaining. 2025 Motability improvements: vehicles now retained during benefit reassessments. If award ends, you may qualify for transitional payment to help with alternative transport.
legal_basis: Motability scheme operates under charity regulations; requires qualifying DLA or PIP mobility component as per Social Security regulations
notes_for_maya: Motability lease includes EVERYTHING: insurance (including young/inexperienced drivers), servicing, MOT, tyres, breakdown cover, road tax. No upfront costs for many vehicles. Some premium vehicles require Advance Payment (one-off fee). October 2025 improvements protect claimants during PIP reassessments - vehicle not withdrawn during review. Particularly valuable for autistic people/families: reduces transport anxiety, enables access to appointments/school/activities, supports independence.
content_excerpt: The Motability Scheme enables disabled people to lease a new car, wheelchair accessible vehicle, scooter or powered wheelchair by exchanging their mobility allowance.

title: EHCP annual review and amendment process for autism
description_plain: Complete guide to EHCP annual reviews and amendments, including legal timelines, what happens at review meetings, and how to request changes for autistic children.
steps_summary:
1. Annual review must happen every 12 months (or 3-6 months for under-5s)
2. School requests reports from professionals, parents, and child (4 weeks before meeting)
3. All reports circulated to attendees (2 weeks before meeting)
4. Attend annual review meeting with school SENCO, professionals, and LA representative
5. Discuss progress, whether provision is working, and any needed changes
6. School submits review report to LA within 2 weeks
7. LA decides within 4 weeks whether to maintain, amend, or cease the EHCP
8. If amended: LA sends draft amended EHCP within 4 weeks of review meeting
9. Parents have 15 days to object or request specific school
10. LA issues final amended EHCP within 12 weeks of review meeting (High Court ruling 2022)
eligibility_summary: All children and young people with an EHCP are entitled to an annual review. Parents, schools, or LA can request early/emergency review if significant change in needs, new diagnosis, or current provision not working. From Year 9 onwards, reviews must include Preparing for Adulthood focus.
evidence_required: updated professional reports (OT, SaLT, EP, medical), parent/carer views in writing, child/young person's views, school progress data, evidence of what's working and what isn't, incident logs or behaviour data if relevant
deadlines: Annual review within 12 months of EHCP issue or last review. LA decision within 4 weeks of review meeting. Draft amended EHCP within 4 weeks, final amended EHCP within 12 weeks total from review meeting. Appeal to SEND Tribunal within 2 months of final amended plan or 1 month from mediation certificate.
legal_basis: Children and Families Act 2014; SEND Code of Practice 2015 Chapter 9; High Court ruling 2022 confirming 12-week amendment timeline
notes_for_maya: Autism is primary need for 30% of pupils with EHCPs - most common diagnosis. Section F provision must be specific and quantified (not vague like 'would benefit from'). LAs often miss statutory deadlines - parents should keep records. Can appeal if LA refuses to amend or makes inadequate changes. IPSEA and local SENDIASS provide free support.
content_excerpt: EHCPs must be reviewed at least once a year. The review meeting considers progress and whether changes are needed to the plan.

title: Appealing EHCP refusal to assess decision
description_plain: How to appeal to SEND Tribunal when your local authority refuses to assess your autistic child for an EHCP, including timelines, evidence needed, and support available.
steps_summary:
1. Receive refusal to assess letter from local authority
2. Contact mediation adviser within 2 months of refusal letter (required - it's free)
3. Decide whether to attend mediation or go straight to appeal (you need certificate either way)
4. Receive mediation certificate
5. Complete Form SEND35a (refusal to assess appeals)
6. Gather all supporting evidence (reports, assessments, school data, parent observations)
7. Submit appeal to SEND Tribunal within 2 months of refusal OR 1 month of mediation certificate (whichever is later)
8. Tribunal assesses on papers (no hearing typically) or you can request oral hearing
9. Await tribunal decision - over 90% of refusal appeals partly successful
eligibility_summary: You can appeal if LA has refused to carry out an EHC needs assessment for your child and you believe they may need specialist provision that only an EHCP can secure. Legal threshold is deliberately low - LA should assess if there's any reasonable possibility child needs EHCP provision. Cannot appeal if LA conducted assessment within previous 6 months.
evidence_required: LA refusal letter, mediation certificate, school reports showing lack of progress despite SEN Support, professional assessments (autism diagnosis, educational psychologist, speech therapy, OT), medical evidence, teacher statements, examples of work, parent observations, private reports if commissioned
deadlines: Contact mediation within 2 months of refusal letter. Submit tribunal appeal within 2 months of refusal letter OR 1 month from mediation certificate date (whichever is later). Tribunal process takes approximately 6+ months total.

contacts.address: First-tier Tribunal (SEND), 1st Floor, Darlington Magistrates Court, Parkgate, Darlington DL1 1RU

legal_basis: Children and Families Act 2014 Part 3; SEND Code of Practice 2015; Tribunal Procedure Rules 2014
notes_for_maya: Over 90% success rate for refusal appeals. No fee to appeal. Legal aid may be available. Free support from IPSEA and local SENDIASS. For autism, emphasise communication, social interaction, sensory needs, and that current SEN Support isn't meeting needs despite intervention. LAs often refuse inappropriately - don't be discouraged.
content_excerpt: You can appeal to the SEND Tribunal if the local authority refuses to carry out an EHC needs assessment for your child.

title: How to appeal to SEND Tribunal for autism EHCP disputes
description_plain: Complete guide to appealing EHCP decisions to the First-tier Tribunal (SEND), including what you can appeal, how to apply, timelines, and what happens at hearings. 45% of appeals involve autism.
steps_summary:
1. Identify what you're appealing (refusal to assess, refusal to issue EHCP, EHCP content Sections B/F/I, refusal to amend/maintain)
2. Contact mediation first if appealing Sections B or F (not needed for Section I school placement only)
3. Obtain mediation certificate (can decline actual mediation and go straight to appeal)
4. Complete Form SEND35 (main appeal form) within deadlines
5. Gather all evidence (LA decision letter, EHCP, medical reports, school records, specialist assessments)
6. Submit to SEND Tribunal by post or email (send@justice.gov.uk)
7. Tribunal acknowledges and requests LA response
8. Exchange evidence with LA via working document
9. Attend hearing (usually virtual video call) with judge and two lay members
10. Receive legally binding decision within weeks of hearing
eligibility_summary: Parents or young people (16+) can appeal LA decisions about EHCPs. Can appeal: refusal to assess, refusal to issue EHCP, EHCP content (needs description, provision, school named), refusal to amend or maintain EHCP. Also can appeal disability discrimination by schools. 98% of cases reaching hearing find in favour of parents. No legal representation required.
evidence_required: LA decision letter, current EHCP if applicable, mediation certificate (if required), all professional reports and assessments, school progress data, medical evidence, autism diagnosis letter, parent statement, child/young person views, evidence of unmet needs, details of preferred school/provision
deadlines: Within 2 months of LA decision for Section I (school) appeals. For Sections B/F: contact mediation within 2 months, then submit appeal within 1 month of mediation certificate OR 2 months of decision (whichever is later). Disability discrimination: within 6 months. NEW 2025: Page limits apply for hearing bundles after 15 July 2025.

contacts.address: First-tier Tribunal (SEND), 1st Floor, Darlington Magistrates Court, Parkgate, Darlington DL1 1RU

legal_basis: Children and Families Act 2014; Tribunal Procedure (First-tier Tribunal) (Special Educational Needs and Disability) Rules 2014; Equality Act 2010 (disability discrimination)
notes_for_maya: Nearly half (45.3%) of SEND Tribunal appeals involve autism - it's the most common primary need. 98% success rate at hearing. NO FEE to appeal. Parents can represent themselves or get free support from IPSEA/SENDIASS. Legal aid available for low income families. Tribunal is less formal than court. Hearings usually virtual since COVID. LA typically has solicitor/barrister but parents often win anyway. Process takes 6+ months total.
breadcrumb: Courts and tribunals › First-tier Tribunal › SEND
content_excerpt: The First-tier Tribunal (Special Educational Needs and Disability) considers appeals against decisions about EHC needs assessments and plans.

title: Free school transport for children with SEND and autism
description_plain: Statutory entitlement to free school transport for children with SEND including autism, covering eligibility criteria, how to apply, types of transport available, and post-16 support.
steps_summary:
1. Check if your child qualifies: has SEND/disability preventing walking to school OR lives beyond statutory distance (2 miles primary, 3 miles secondary)
2. Confirm school is 'qualifying school' (state school or independent school named in EHCP Section I)
3. Contact your local council's school transport team to request application form
4. Complete application providing EHCP details, school placement confirmation, medical evidence if applicable
5. Council assesses individual needs and determines suitable transport type
6. Provide travel passport information (child's specific needs, sensitivities, communication preferences)
7. Receive transport allocation (taxi with escort, minibus, personal transport budget, or travel training)
8. For post-16: reapply annually, may require means-tested contribution unless on low-income benefits
eligibility_summary: Child cannot reasonably walk to school due to SEND, disability, or mobility problem (no distance requirement), OR standard distance criteria apply (over 2 miles for primary, 3 miles for secondary, or 2 miles secondary for low-income families). School must be nearest appropriate qualifying school. Transport must be suitable for child's individual needs and enable non-stressful journey.
evidence_required: EHCP (if applicable), proof of school placement, medical evidence of disability/mobility needs, evidence of distance from school if using distance criteria, low-income benefit evidence for post-16 free transport
deadlines: Apply before start of school year for September transport (deadlines typically March-June, varies by council). Late applications accepted but no guarantee transport ready for term start. Post-16 applications must be renewed annually. Processing: 2 weeks for in-year, up to 8 weeks for advance applications.
legal_basis: Education Act 1996 Section 508B; Home to School Travel and Transport Statutory Guidance 2014; Equality Act 2010
notes_for_maya: 'Cannot reasonably walk' is assessed individually for autism - considers sensory sensitivities, safety risks, need for adult supervision, whether journey would cause distress. Transport must be suitable: may include taxi with trained escort, personal transport budget (PTB) for parents to arrange, or travel training for more independent young people. Post-16 transport often requires contribution (around £270/term) unless on qualifying low-income benefits. Eligibility and policies vary by local council.
content_excerpt: If your child has special educational needs or a disability, they may be entitled to free school transport.

title: Preparing for Adulthood: Transition planning for autistic young people (16-25)
description_plain: Complete guide to transition planning from children's to adult services for autistic young people aged 16-25 with EHCPs, covering education, employment, independent living, and health.
steps_summary:
1. Transition planning formally starts in Year 9 (age 13-14) EHCP annual reviews
2. Invite Adult Social Care Transition Team social worker to Year 9 review
3. Discuss four Preparing for Adulthood outcomes: employment, independent living, health, community participation
4. Age 14+: Request Care Act assessment if need for adult social care likely
5. Age 16-17: Complete Care Act assessment and post-16 EHCP transfer process
6. Visit colleges/training providers early (Year 10-11) for post-16 options
7. Develop life skills: travel training, money management, work experience
8. Age 18: Transition to adult services, review decision-making capacity, apply for Universal Credit for disabled students
9. Age 18-25: EHCP continues if in education/training, can extend support to age 25
10. Person-centred planning throughout - focus on young person's aspirations and strengths
eligibility_summary: All young people aged 16-25 with EHCPs are entitled to Preparing for Adulthood planning. Those with autism likely to need ongoing support in adulthood should receive Care Act assessment from age 16-17. EHCP can continue to age 25 if in education or training. Adult social care available if eligible under Care Act (substantial difficulty in daily living and unable to achieve wellbeing outcomes).
evidence_required: current EHCP, professional reports (OT, SaLT, psychology), autism diagnosis, evidence of support needs in employment/education/living/health, school reports, young person's views and aspirations, parent/carer views
deadlines: Transition planning from Year 9 (age 13-14). Care Act assessment should be completed by age 17-18. Annual EHCP reviews continue throughout. Post-16 EHCP transfer by 31 March for September college start. EHCP ceases at age 25 or when young person leaves education/training.
legal_basis: Children and Families Act 2014; Care Act 2014; SEND Code of Practice 2015 Chapter 8; Mental Capacity Act 2005
notes_for_maya: Four PfA outcomes: Employment, Independent Living, Health, Community/Relationships. Post-16 options include FE colleges, sixth form, apprenticeships, supported employment, day services. Decision-making capacity becomes relevant at 18 - Mental Capacity Act applies. Universal Credit available for disabled students if LCWRA established before course. Support can extend to 25 for autistic people in education/training. Free support from SENDIASS and local transition teams.
content_excerpt: Preparing for Adulthood supports young people with SEND to have choice and control over their lives as they become adults.

title: How to request a Care Act assessment for autistic adults
description_plain: Legal right to free needs assessment under Care Act 2014 for autistic adults. Assessment determines what support is needed for daily living, wellbeing, and achieving personal outcomes.
steps_summary:
1. Contact your local authority adult social care department (duty team)
2. Request a Care Act Assessment - state clearly 'I would like to request a Care Act Assessment'
3. Explain you have autism diagnosis and why you need support (daily living difficulties, managing routines, social situations)
4. Optionally submit written request using NAS letter template
5. Wait for social worker assignment (follow up if no contact within 6 weeks)
6. Prepare for assessment: list difficulties, gather evidence, bring support person
7. Complete assessment (one or multiple visits) - be honest about all challenges
8. Receive eligibility decision and support plan if eligible
9. Financial assessment may follow to determine contribution based on income
eligibility_summary: All autistic adults in England are entitled to free Care Act assessment regardless of IQ level or other disabilities. Formal autism diagnosis required. Assessment evaluates how autism affects daily living and what support could help achieve wellbeing outcomes. No presumption about ability to manage - assessment must be autism-informed.
evidence_required: autism diagnosis letter, evidence of daily living difficulties, medical reports, current support arrangements, list of autism-related expenses (sensory needs, food, transport), examples of challenges with bills/shopping/travel/social situations
deadlines: No legal time limit for assessment but local authorities should respond promptly. Follow up if no contact within 6 weeks. Assessment should happen as soon as reasonably practicable. Can request urgent/emergency assessment if crisis situation.
legal_basis: Care Act 2014 Section 9; Care and Support Statutory Guidance 2014 (updated 2023); Autism Act 2009
notes_for_maya: Care Act 2014 requires assessors to have autism knowledge or consult someone who does. Right to advocacy support. No means test for assessment itself (only for funding support). Can lead to Personal Budget, Direct Payments, or council-arranged services. Emphasize right to person-centred, autism-informed assessment focusing on outcomes not just tasks.
breadcrumb: Social care › England › Assessments and care plans
content_excerpt: Under the Care Act 2014, autistic adults have a legal right to a free needs assessment from their local authority to determine what support they require.

title: Carer's assessment under Care Act 2014 for autism carers
description_plain: Free assessment of support needs for unpaid carers of autistic adults or children. Focuses on carer's wellbeing, ability and willingness to continue caring, and impact on work, education, and social life.
steps_summary:
1. Contact your local council adult social care duty team
2. Request a 'carer's assessment' specifically
3. Explain the autistic person's needs and your caring role (hours, tasks, impact)
4. You can request assessment even if autistic person hasn't had needs assessment
5. Prepare evidence: document daily care tasks, sleep disruption, social isolation, work/education impact, health effects
6. Attend assessment meeting (can bring support person or advocate)
7. Assessor evaluates whether you're able and willing to care, impact on wellbeing, outcomes you want
8. Receive eligibility decision based on national criteria
9. If eligible, receive support plan and funding (Personal Budget, Direct Payments, or council services)
10. May require financial assessment contribution based on your income
eligibility_summary: All adult carers (18+) providing care for autistic adults are entitled to assessment under Care Act 2014. Parent carers of autistic children assessed under Children and Families Act 2014. No requirement that autistic person has formal diagnosis. Council cannot assume you're willing/able to care. Assessment must consider impact on physical/emotional health, work, education, relationships, and whether support could help you achieve your outcomes.
evidence_required: details of caring role and hours provided, evidence of impact on wellbeing (sleep logs, missed work/social activities, stress), autism-related challenges you help manage (prompting, emotional regulation, supervision), additional costs incurred, examples of tasks autistic person would manage if not autistic
deadlines: No legal deadline but council should assess promptly. Request urgent assessment if crisis. Assessment outcomes remain valid until circumstances change. Can request review if needs increase. Care Act allows preventative assessments before crisis develops.
legal_basis: Care Act 2014 Sections 10-11; Children and Families Act 2014 (parent carers); Care and Support Statutory Guidance Chapter 6
notes_for_maya: Care Act 2014 gives carers equal rights to assessment as those cared for (first time in law). Assessor must have autism knowledge/experience. Council cannot assume family will provide care for free. Support can include respite, training, equipment, signposting. No presumption about carer capacity. Emphasize carer's wellbeing is paramount and support should enable them to achieve outcomes (employment, education, social participation).
breadcrumb: Social care › England › Carers › Carers assessments
content_excerpt: Under the Care Act 2014, carers have equal legal rights to assessment and support as the people they care for.

title: Direct Payments for autism adult social care
description_plain: Cash payments from local authorities allowing autistic adults to arrange and manage their own social care support instead of receiving council-arranged services. Provides flexibility and control over care delivery.
steps_summary:
1. Request Care Act assessment from local authority adult social care team
2. Be assessed as eligible for social care support
3. Receive Personal Budget calculation based on assessed needs
4. Choose to receive funding as Direct Payments (alternative to council-arranged services)
5. Set up bank account for Direct Payments if required
6. Create support plan showing how money will be used according to care needs
7. Decide what to purchase: personal assistants, specialist autism services, day activities, equipment, respite
8. If employing PAs: set up as employer (PAYE, insurance, contracts) or use payroll service
9. Council monitors spending to ensure alignment with care plan
10. Submit regular reports/receipts as required by local authority
11. Undergo financial assessment to determine contribution based on income
eligibility_summary: Available to autistic adults (18+) assessed as needing social care support under Care Act 2014. Must have mental capacity to manage payments or have representative/managed account service. Can be used to employ personal assistants (including family members who don't live with you), purchase specialist autism services, equipment, day services, respite care. Rates vary by local authority (typically £15-27/hour in 2025).
evidence_required: Care Act assessment confirming eligible needs, support plan detailing how Direct Payments will be used, bank details, if employing staff: employer insurance and PAYE setup, financial information for means-tested contribution assessment
deadlines: Apply for Care Act assessment first (no deadline but recommended ASAP). Direct Payments option offered during care planning. Financial assessment follows eligibility decision. Must use payments according to care plan. Submit monitoring reports as required by council (typically quarterly or annually).
legal_basis: Care Act 2014 Sections 31-33; Care and Support (Direct Payments) Regulations 2014; National Living Wage Act 2016
notes_for_maya: Direct Payments hourly rates vary significantly by council (2025: £16.65-£26.18 typically). PA employment requires PAYE, insurance, contracts - managed account services can handle this. Autism-specific uses: sensory-friendly activities, social communication support, routine maintenance. Cannot be used for council's legal duties (reasonable adjustments employer must provide). Financial contribution based on income/savings means-test. Emphasize flexibility and person-centred control.
breadcrumb: Social care › England › Paying for social care
content_excerpt: Direct Payments give you more choice and control over the care and support you receive.

title: Respite care and short breaks for autistic adults and carers
description_plain: Temporary relief for family carers of autistic adults through short breaks, residential respite facilities, sitting services, and day activities. Allows carers to rest and recharge while autistic person receives support in safe environment.
steps_summary:
1. Contact local authority adult social care department
2. Request Carer's Assessment (free, legal right under Care Act 2014)
3. Autistic person should also have Needs Assessment
4. Based on assessments, council recommends suitable respite services
5. Discuss funding options: council-funded, Personal Budget/Direct Payments, or self-funded
6. Choose type of respite: at home (sitting service, overnight PA), away from home (residential facility, Shared Lives), or day services
7. Identify providers: search Autism Services Directory, contact local council for Short Breaks Services Statement, or use postcode search
8. Arrange dates and book respite with chosen provider
9. Provide information about autistic person's needs, communication, sensory preferences, routines
10. Regular reviews ensure respite remains suitable and effective
eligibility_summary: Available to carers of autistic adults who need temporary relief from caring responsibilities. Assessment under Care Act 2014 determines eligibility. No need to wait for crisis - preventative support encouraged. Respite does not affect benefits for autistic person or carer. Funding depends on assessment outcomes and financial means-test. Some areas offer 'universal short breaks' without assessment.
evidence_required: carer's assessment, autistic person's needs assessment, evidence of caring role and impact on wellbeing, information about autistic person's support needs/communication/sensory preferences, financial information if means-tested contribution required
deadlines: Request assessments as soon as need for respite identified. No legal deadline for assessment but council should respond promptly (follow up if no contact within 6 weeks). Respite can be one-off, regular (weekly/monthly), or emergency. Bookings subject to provider availability.
legal_basis: Care Act 2014; Chronically Sick and Disabled Persons Act 1970; Children Act 1989 Section 17 (under-18s)
notes_for_maya: UK providers include Dimensions UK (5 locations), National Autistic Society (UK-wide), Autism East Midlands Whitegates (Nottinghamshire), Curam Care (home-based UK-wide). Check local council Short Breaks Services Statement for area-specific options. Respite types: sitting service (few hours), overnight PA, residential facility (days/weeks), Shared Lives placement, day services. Support organizations: Scope, Carers UK, Carers Trust. Legislation: Care Act gives carers equal rights to support.
breadcrumb: Advice and support › Respite care and short breaks
content_excerpt: Respite care provides temporary relief for carers. It gives you a break from caring while the person you care for gets support.

title: Access to Work grant for autistic employees and self-employed
description_plain: Government grant providing practical support and funding for autistic people to start work, stay in work, or become self-employed. Covers support workers, specialist equipment, travel costs, workplace assessments, and autism awareness training. Grant doesn't need repaying and won't affect other benefits.
steps_summary:
1. Check eligibility: have autism affecting ability to work, employed/self-employed/have job offer, work 1+ hours/week, age 16+
2. Apply online at gov.uk/access-to-work/apply OR call 0800 121 7479 (Monday-Friday 9am-5pm)
3. For new job: apply up to 6 weeks before start date. For self-employed: have Unique Taxpayer Reference ready
4. During application: explain clearly how autism impacts specific work tasks (e.g., sensory overload, social communication, organisation)
5. Describe what support would help (quiet workspace, job coach, visual schedules, communication aids)
6. Case manager contacts you within few weeks to discuss needs
7. Workplace assessment may occur (phone/video/in-person) to determine tailored support
8. Receive award decision and written agreement outlining approved support and funding
9. Access to Work covers costs from application date (apply before incurring expenses)
10. Awards last up to 3 years; reapply 12 weeks before support ends
eligibility_summary: Autistic people who are employed, self-employed, or about to start work (with written job offer), working at least 1 hour per week, age 16+, in England/Scotland/Wales. No formal diagnosis required but must show autism has substantial long-term impact on work. Cannot cover 'reasonable adjustments' employers must provide under Equality Act 2010. Covers support workers, equipment, travel, workplace assessments, communication support, autism awareness training for colleagues.
evidence_required: evidence of employment or job offer, description of how autism affects work tasks, details of support needed, for self-employed: Unique Taxpayer Reference and business records/invoices, employer information (optional but helpful)
deadlines: Apply up to 6 weeks before starting new job. For existing jobs, apply as soon as support needed. Funding only starts from application date (no backdating). Awards typically last 3 years. Access to Work contacts you 12 weeks before support ends for renewal. Annual cap approximately £68,800.
legal_basis: Equality Act 2010; Access to Work scheme regulations; National Living Wage Act 2016
notes_for_maya: Access to Work funds: support worker/job coach (organisation, planning, workload management, social communication), specialist equipment (software, noise-cancelling headphones), travel costs if can't use public transport, workplace assessments, communication support, autism awareness training for colleagues/managers, mental health support related to work. Cost sharing: new job first 6 weeks 100% funded; after 6 weeks small employers pay 20%, medium/large employers pay first £500-£1000 then 20% up to £10k, Access to Work covers above £10k in full. Get help from Employment Autism charity.
content_excerpt: Access to Work can help you get or stay in work if you have a physical or mental health condition or disability. The support you get will depend on your needs.

title: Disabled Person's Freedom Pass application in Hounslow
description_plain: Free travel pass for Hounslow disabled residents providing free bus, tube, train, tram, and river services across London. Automatic eligibility for certain disability benefits, standard eligibility based on mobility/sensory impairments including autism-related difficulties.
steps_summary:
1. Check eligibility: Hounslow resident with disability affecting ability to walk, use of arms, or sensory impairment (deaf/blind)
2. Automatic qualification if you receive: Higher Rate Mobility DLA, War Pensioner's Mobility Supplement, qualifying Armed Forces Compensation
3. For standard eligibility: gather medical evidence (GP/consultant letters, hospital reports, prescriptions, Blue Badge details if applicable)
4. Download Disabled Person's Freedom Pass application form from Hounslow Council website
5. Complete all sections fully including personal details, disability information, transport usage
6. Submit form with all supporting medical documentation
7. You may be asked to attend assessment appointment (refusal may result in cancellation)
8. Await decision and processing (passes arrive within 7 working days if approved by post)
9. If rejected, you have 28 days from rejection letter to submit appeal
10. Keep pass valid - borough may review periodically to confirm ongoing eligibility
eligibility_summary: Permanent Hounslow residents with disability/injury causing substantial long-term adverse effects on ability to walk, OR no arms/long-term loss of use of both arms, OR profoundly/severely deaf, OR registered severely sight impaired (blind). Automatic for Higher Rate Mobility DLA, War Pensioner's Mobility Supplement, Armed Forces Compensation. For autism, eligibility based on functional impact on mobility/ability to navigate public transport safely (sensory overload, anxiety, safety awareness).
evidence_required: proof of Hounslow residency, medical documentation (GP/consultant letters, hospital reports, diagnosis letters), prescription copies if taking medication, Blue Badge details if issued within 6 months (first 6 digits and expiry date), evidence of functional impact on mobility/travel ability
deadlines: Apply as soon as eligible. No application fee (service is free - Hounslow does not charge). Passes that expired March 31, 2025 sent to eligible renewal holders. If address changed, contact new borough to discuss renewal. Processing: passes arrive within 7 working days by post if approved. Appeals: 28 days from rejection letter date.
legal_basis: Greater London Authority Act 1999; London Local Authorities Act 2000; Equality Act 2010
notes_for_maya: Hounslow-specific application. Freedom Pass provides free travel on ALL London transport: buses, tubes, trains, trams, DLR, Overground, TfL Rail, river services. For autism, focus on functional impact: sensory overload in crowds/stations, difficulty navigating complex routes, safety awareness issues, anxiety using public transport. Medical evidence crucial if not automatically eligible. Missing documentation delays application. Pass valid as long as eligibility criteria met. Different from older person's Freedom Pass (60+).
content_excerpt: The Disabled Person's Freedom Pass provides free travel on buses, tubes, trains, trams and river services in London for eligible disabled Hounslow residents.
